title: Introduction to the Moral Law
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: 05-14-23 - Intro to Moral Law - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: Humanity as Moral Creatures Made in God's Image
subpoints: Genesis 1:26-27 — all humanity bears the image of God, not just Adam and Eve
Angels and humans are the two categories of moral creatures
Adam and Eve were not neutral — they were originally righteous
The moral law is written in every human heart; no one is without it

outline.title: What Is the Moral Law? (WLC Q. 93)
subpoints: The moral law is the declaration of the will of God to mankind
It binds everyone to personal, perfect, and perpetual conformity and obedience
It encompasses body and soul, duties to God and to men
It promises life upon fulfilling and threatens death upon breach
Obedience is required by God's covenant (Deuteronomy 5, 31-32)

outline.title: Implications of the Moral Law
subpoints: All humanity owes obedience to God — not just Christians
Humanity's primary duty is to God; serving others is a service to God
We have no right to exempt ourselves from any part of God's revealed will
The kingdom of God is an absolute monarchy, not a democracy
Before the fall, the law was written on hearts; after the fall, we need the written word
The moral law is more than the Ten Commandments — it spans Genesis through Revelation
God requires perfect obedience, not just "goodness" — this points us to Christ

outline.title: Only Christ Fulfills the Law for Us
subpoints: Only those who fulfill the law perfectly can have fellowship with God and eternal life
No one can achieve this; God provided the second Adam — the Lord Jesus Christ
Christ's perfect life is imputed to us — not just his death, but his life
His death removes guilt; his perfect life is credited as our righteousness
The only option: trust in Christ or perish

summary:
Pastor Tito Lyro introduces the first lesson in a series on the moral law of God, using the Westminster Larger Catechism as a guide. He begins with the foundational truth that all humanity is created in the image of God and is therefore inherently moral — no person is neutral before God. The moral law is not a later addition but was written on the hearts of all humanity at creation. After the fall, the darkness of the human heart makes the written Word necessary, since sinful nature inevitably suppresses the truth of the law written within.

Drawing from WLC Question 93, he defines the moral law as "the declaration of the will of God to mankind, directing and binding everyone to personal, perfect, and perpetual conformity and obedience." Several implications flow from this: all humanity owes obedience to God; humanity's primary duty is God-directed; no one may exempt himself from any part of God's revealed will; and God requires not just goodness but absolute moral perfection — a standard that cannot be lowered without eliminating the need for Christ.

The lesson concludes with the gospel truth: since no one since Adam has fulfilled the law perfectly, the only avenue to fellowship with God is through the Lord Jesus Christ, the second Adam. His perfect life — not just his atoning death — is imputed to believers. The death of Christ removes guilt; the life of Christ provides the positive righteousness God requires. Pastor Lyro is emphatic that the standard remains perfection and that only Christ meets it on our behalf.
transcript:
## Humanity as Moral Creatures

As they are in Exodus 20 and Deuteronomy 5. So just to cover a basic — man is created in the image of God with a moral nature. No one is neutral, right? So Genesis 1:26 and 27 tells us that we are created in God's image. And that's not just Adam and Eve — it's the entirety of creation. We could go through the description and show that this is applied to humanity, not just to the first parents. We see that in Ecclesiastes, we see that in Amos, we see that in Jonah and so on. We see that in the Gospel of Matthew as well. So we're created in God's image, which means that we are moral creatures. Nobody's neutral, period. Adam and Eve weren't neutral. We tend to think of Adam and Eve as these neutral people that could go this way or could go that way — no. They were righteous. They were intrinsically, inherently righteous.

And that's all humanity is moral. So there are two categories of moral creatures. So humanity is one — what's the other one? Angels. Angels and humans are moral. So dogs and cats and whatever your preferred pet, they're not moral. They don't have souls. They don't go to heaven when they die. I'm so sorry. And they will not participate in the resurrection. Now that's not to say that there won't be animals in the eternal state, because they're God's creatures also. But your pet is not going to heaven with you when you pass.

So the Scripture teaches that not only is that specific command given to not eat of the fruit of the tree of the knowledge of good and evil, but the moral law is also given to Adam and Eve. And we saw that in the passage that we read this morning to begin with — the idea of the moral law written in the heart of humanity. So God in creating humanity built into the heart of humanity the law of God, the moral law. So there's no person who ever lived, who lives today, or who ever will live, that is not aware of what God requires of them. That person does not exist. That's what the teaching of Romans 2 is — showing how the Gentiles are also guilty of sin, even if they have never heard the word of God, because God had built in them his law and we call that often our conscience. Now that can be seared to the point that we don't listen to it anymore, but that is our own sin against God's image in us.

We see in this question that the standard of righteousness is the moral law of God. That's how we measure what is true and what is right and what's wrong. When we talk about morality, which we think of as right and wrong, the standard that we judge everything by to say if it's right or wrong is the moral law of God. And Paul says, "for Moses writes about the righteousness which is of the law: the man who does these things shall live by them." If you can keep the law of God, you're going to live. That's going to be your basis of a relationship with God. Now just kind of scenes of the next episode, no one can do that. There's not a possibility left on the table. And that's really the argument that Paul's making in Romans chapter 10.

## What Is the Moral Law? (WLC Q. 93)

The answer also teaches that God did give this special command to Adam and Eve to not eat of the tree of the knowledge of good and evil. And do you notice, do you remember how the context only had that particular verse — from verse 5, I think to verse 16, of chapter 2, God talked about all the great things that Adam and Eve could do. And then this is one verse — verse 17 — this is the one thing you can't do. When chapter 3 comes and Satan comes to tempt Adam and Eve, does he refer to all the great things that God said, "this is for you, everything else"? A billion things for you, this is the one thing. But Satan says, "Oh, God is keeping good things from you. See this one thing, he really is afraid that you might become like him. That's why he's doing that." And I think our minds often work that way instead of looking at all the things that God has provided — the positive commands of God, the blessings of God, the blessings of obedience — we focus on the things that God commands us not to do.

Any questions before we continue? All right, question 93 then asks, what is the moral law? Well, the moral law is the declaration of the will of God to mankind, directing and binding everyone to personal, perfect, and perpetual conformity and obedience there unto in the frame and disposition of the whole man, soul and body, and in performance of all those duties of holiness and righteousness which he owes to God and men, promising life upon the fulfilling and threatening death upon the breach of it.

So that's the moral law and its application there. Just to again cover the obvious: God's moral law requires obedience. In Deuteronomy 5, right before we are given the Ten Commandments, it says, "Moses called all Israel and said to them, hear the statutes and judgments which I speak in your hearing today that you may learn them and be careful to observe them. The Lord our God made a covenant with us" — and there is the idea of obedience.

And then we also saw that God's law is a revelation of God's will. In Deuteronomy 31-32, Moses says, "But as for you, stand here by me — God speaking through Moses — and I will speak to you all the commandments, the statutes, and the judgments which you shall teach them." God equates the moral law with his will.

Do you notice that the answer says that the law requires conforming of the whole person, not just the soul, not just the body, but body and soul. And Jesus addresses that in Luke chapter 10, where he's challenged by the teacher of the law. There, he answers, he says that the summary of the law, the great commandment, the two great commandments, is the Lord your God — and then see how he uses three or four words there to define how: strength, or body depending on what Gospel you read, mind, soul, and so on. So the whole person is involved in loving God, and we're already learning that loving God is demonstrated by obeying his word.

And that the law also teaches us that the law of God requires total and perfect obedience, and that's the view of the point that Paul's making in Romans and Galatians. That sure, you're going to say that your relationship with God is based on your performance? Then here's the standard: everything. Not in a moment, but every moment. Not one time, but all at the same time. And the law of God is binding on all elements of the human personality. Paul says, "Now may the God of peace himself sanctify you completely and may your whole spirit, soul and body, be preserved blameless at the coming of our Lord Jesus Christ." So here he associates sanctification, which is a growth in ability in obedience, to every element of the human person.

I think I'm going to skip the next couple of slides because they're just more of what we have been talking about, and just close this section of our lesson with this idea that death is the penalty involved in breaking God's law. And we see that from Adam in Genesis chapter 3, and we find that traced throughout the history of the Scriptures.

## Implications

Any questions or comments before we now take a few minutes to talk about some implications from the answers of questions 91, 92, and 93? All right, so first implication: all humanity owes obedience to God because he created all things. Not just some, but all humanity. We tend to think that what God commands in the Bible is only for Christians. But that's not the case. This is the standard for everyone who descends from Adam, as Adam was to obey God. So do all his descendants, and that's why everyone is guilty of their own sin, in addition to being fully guilty of original sin. Because that commitment to obey God applies to everyone.

The second implication is humanity's primary duty is to God. And if you think of all people who ever lived, who live today, who ever lived, their primary duty is to God. We serve other people as a service to God. So that's why humanism and humanitarianism are not to be praised, because serving others is not an end in and of itself. Why is it that we love our neighbor? We love our neighbor because he bears the image of God. So really, if you think about it, there's only one great commitment. It's to love God, because what we're loving in other people is the image of God in them. So that's why when we dig wells in Africa and so on — generally good things — our primary duty is not to the people who don't have water, but to the God who created those people who don't have water. And that's the right attitude that we're to have in doing those things. We serve our fellow men, not just for their sake, but for God's. And if we serve our fellow men for their sake only, we have become idolaters.

Another implication: we don't have the right to choose not to obey God's revealed will. We can't say, "I'm going to exempt myself from obedience to this particular command, or that particular command, or this particular part of the Bible." Unless the Bible itself says that you're exempt from that. We just don't have the right to do that.

Another implication: the kingdom of God is not a democracy, but an absolute monarchy. Now often they say it's a constitutional monarchy, and it is — but as a constitution, it was created not by a committee of people, but by the monarchy himself, God, in giving us His Word. So God doesn't have to ask your or my opinion about what He requires of us. We don't get to choose if we like them or not.

Another implication: before the fall, humanity could follow all the commandments without them being written out. In Genesis 1 and 2, we don't have the Ten Commandments listed, because that wasn't necessary — they could perfectly follow the law written in their heart. After the fall, the fall itself darkened and distorted the heart to the point that this natural revelation of the will of God is no longer adequate to guide us. Apart from the light of Scripture, humanity inevitably changes the truth of God into a lie and worships and serves the creature more than the Creator, as we see in Romans 1:25.

Another implication: the moral law is more than the Ten Commandments. The Ten Commandments summarize it, but it's more than the Ten Commandments. The Larger Catechism says that the moral law is the declaration of the will of God to mankind. So it spans Genesis 1 through Revelation 22.

Next to last implication: God requires perfect obedience to his law. It is important that we don't just think that God requires us to be good, because we have a distorted idea of what good is. We think of good as a balance between what we do — if we do more good things than evil things, we are good. But God doesn't require us to be good. He requires us to be perfect, which is a different concept. When you think God requires you to be good, you're actually lowering God's standard. You're making it reachable, much like what the Pharisees did in 1st century Judaism. So God's standard is not merely goodness, but absolute moral perfection all the time. And this is an incredibly high standard, and it must be so. This standard keeps us reminded of our need of Christ. If we lower it, we don't need Christ anymore.

## Only Christ Fulfills the Law for Us

And then the last one: only those who fulfill the law perfectly can have fellowship with God and eternal life. What? That just means we are going to hell. But this is absolutely true. Only those who fulfill the law perfectly can have fellowship with God and eternal life. That was true of Adam and Eve in the state of innocency, and it's true throughout history. That's the standard. It cannot be lowered. It will not be changed. The basis for fellowship with God still remains the covenant of works. But God himself provided the second Adam, the Lord Jesus Christ, on our behalf as our representative, so that by the obedience of one shall many be made righteous.

So the requirement is still perfection. God provided one to be perfect for us, so that when we believe in him, his perfection is counted as ours. That's why we need to emphasize not only the death of Christ on the cross, but also his life. It is that life that's counted as our perfection. Think about this: the death on the cross eliminates our guilt. But that brings us to zero. And God is not interested in neutral people. He's interested in perfect people. And that's why the life of Christ is so important, because that perfect life is then counted as ours. And we are not just standing as forgiven — but not guilty as well, at the same time. Not guilty of ever committing sin. And above that, even more than that, we are declared innocent, and positively righteous, as having obeyed the entirety of God's moral law.

So keep that in mind: only those who fulfill the law perfectly can have fellowship with God and eternal life. And that's why the only avenue left for you is the Lord Jesus Christ. That or hell. There's no third option in life for anyone.

Any questions? Andrew: recommended resources? Yes, Johannes Vos, commentary on the Larger Catechism. It's a fat book, but don't let it scare you — he breaks each question into several smaller questions and applies them. Very helpful resource. And then if you want the baby version, you can also use G.I. Williamson's study notes on the Shorter Catechism. This will be on volume two. We have that in our library as well.

All right. So let's pray and then we'll dismiss. Father, thank you for your goodness to us. We thank you that you are a God who revealed yourself to us in your word. We thank you for writing your law on hearts. And we thank you for opening our eyes to see it in Jesus Christ. We thank you for his perfection. We pray that we cling to his perfection, not ours. Since ours doesn't exist. And even as we worship you this morning, we pray that we would be able to come before you because of Christ and through him. We're asking in his name, amen.
